mnemonics.title: Serializability Check
mnemonics.content: Precedence Graph: Draw nodes for each transaction, draw arrows when conflicts occur, check for cycles - if NO cycles, schedule is serializable (CYCLE-FREE = SERIALIZABLE)

mnemonics.title: Schedule Properties Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: STRICT → CASCADELESS → RECOVERABLE. Think: Strictly Clean Records are kept. Strict is hardest, Recoverable is easiest to satisfy.

mnemonics.title: Conflict Types Remember
mnemonics.content: WW, RW, WR - these CONFLICT. Only WR or RW or WW. RR never conflicts. Remember: Read-Read is Friendly (no conflict).

acronyms.usage: One transaction reads while another writes the same item
One transaction writes while another reads the same item
Two transactions write to the same data item

visualTips.visualization: Draw each transaction as a circle (T1, T2, etc.). For each pair of conflicting operations (where one writes), draw an arrow FROM the transaction that executed first TO the one that executed second. The arrow shows 'happened-before' relationship.
Think of a pyramid: Top (narrow) = Strict, Middle = Cascadeless, Bottom (wide) = Recoverable. All strict are cascadeless, all cascadeless are recoverable.
Use a 2x2 matrix: Rows are operations (R/W), Columns are operations (R/W). Only combinations with at least one W conflict: (R,W), (W,R), (W,W). The (R,R) cell is safe/no conflict.

analogies.analogy: Think of a restaurant kitchen. If two chefs prepare dishes in serial (one after another), the result is predictable. Serializability ensures interleaved cooking (concurrent transactions) produces the same final meal as if they cooked one after another.
Two people trying to edit the same Google Doc simultaneously - they conflict. One reading while another edits causes problems. Only when both are just viewing (reading) can they work together safely.
Think of a family tree showing who was born first. If there's a cycle (A is parent of B, B is parent of A), something is wrong. No cycles means proper ordering exists.

In a group project, you should only submit (commit) your work after everyone in your group has submitted theirs. Otherwise, if someone drops out (rolls back), your submission is based on incomplete work.
